root_text.sn56.54:1.1: “Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, yatthimā mahānadiyo saṁsandanti samenti, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn56.54:1.2: gaṅgā, yamunā, aciravatī, sarabhū, mahī, taṁ udakaṁ parikkhayaṁ pariyādānaṁ gaccheyya, ṭhapetvā dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni.
root_text.sn56.54:1.4: katamaṁ nu kho bahutaraṁ—yaṁ vā saṁbhejjaudakaṁ parikkhīṇaṁ pariyādinnaṁ, yāni dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni avasiṭṭhānī”ti?
root_text.sn56.54:1.5: “Etadeva, bhante, bahutaraṁ saṁbhejjaudakaṁ yadidaṁ parikkhīṇaṁ pariyādinnaṁ; appamattakāni dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni avasiṭṭhāni.
root_text.sn56.54:1.6: Saṅkhampi na upenti, upanidhampi na upenti, kalabhāgampi na upenti saṁbhejjaudakaṁ parikkhīṇaṁ pariyādinnaṁ upanidhāya dve vā tīṇi vā udakaphusitāni avasiṭṭhānī”ti.

translation_text.sn56.54:1.1: “Mendicants, there are places where the great rivers—the Ganges, Yamuna, Aciravatī, Sarabhū, and Mahī—join together and converge.
translation_text.sn56.54:1.2: Suppose that water dried up and evaporated except for two or three drops.
translation_text.sn56.54:1.4: Which is more: the water in the confluence that has dried up and evaporated, or the two or three drops left?”
translation_text.sn56.54:1.5: “Sir, the water in the confluence that has dried up and evaporated is certainly more. The two or three drops left are tiny.
translation_text.sn56.54:1.6: Compared to the water in the confluence that has dried up and evaporated, it doesn’t count, there’s no comparison, it’s not worth a fraction.”
translation_text.sn56.54:1.7: “In the same way, for a noble disciple …
translation_text.sn56.54:1.8: That’s why you should practice meditation …”
